content.text: FlashAttention-1 established the IO-aware tiling algorithm and proved that eliminating HBM round-trips for the N×N attention matrix delivers dramatic speedups. But FA-1 left substantial performance on the table: it achieved roughly 35% of A100 theoretical peak throughput. FlashAttention-2 and FlashAttention-3 are successive hardware-aware refinements — FA-2 nearly doubled throughput through algorithmic restructuring (less non-matmul work, better parallelism) while FA-3 exploits H100-specific hardware features (Tensor Memory Accelerator, warpgroup instructions) to push throughput near the hardware ceiling.

attrs.alt: Architecture comparison of FlashAttention-1, FlashAttention-2, and FlashAttention-3 showing parallelism strategy, warp organization, and throughput
attrs.title: FlashAttention Evolution: FA-1 → FA-2 → FA-3

content.text: FA-2 (Dao, 2023) addresses three specific performance gaps in FA-1, each targeting a different layer of the GPU execution model:

On A100 GPUs, tensor-core matrix multiplications run at 312 TFLOPS (FP16), but scalar arithmetic (additions, exponentials, divisions) runs at only 19 TFLOPS. Every scalar operation is ~16× more expensive per FLOP in wall-clock terms. FA-1's inner loop divided the output accumulator by the running sum
at each step, performing two rescale multiplications per K/V column block. FA-2 restructures the loop to accumulate unnormalized outputs throughout and perform the final
division only once at the end of the outer loop. The number of division/rescale operations drops from

FA-1 parallelized across the batch × head dimension only. An A100 has 108 SMs. If a model has batch_size=1 and 32 heads, only 32 thread blocks run concurrently — leaving 76 SMs idle. FA-2 adds parallelism over the sequence dimension: the outer loop over Q row blocks is distributed across independent thread blocks. With 32 heads and 16 sequence partitions, 512 thread blocks run simultaneously, saturating all 108 SMs. This change is critical for inference, where batch size is often 1.
Within a thread block, FA-1 assigned all warps to the same Q tile, each computing parts of
. FA-2 instead partitions different K/V column blocks across warps: warp 0 processes

, then a synchronization step combines the per-warp accumulators using the online softmax merge formula. This reduces the number of shared-memory reads and eliminates intra-warp communication bottlenecks in FA-1.
FA-3 (Shah, Günnemann, Dao, 2024) is designed specifically for NVIDIA's Hopper architecture (H100). It exploits three H100-specific capabilities unavailable on Ampere:

H100 includes dedicated hardware units that copy data between HBM and SRAM asynchronously, without occupying compute thread registers. FA-3 uses TMA to overlap data movement with computation: while consumer warps compute
for the current tile, TMA is simultaneously loading
into a second SRAM buffer (double-buffering). This pipeline hides HBM latency behind computation, which was a significant source of idle time in FA-2.

Hopper introduces a new instruction that operates on groups of 4 warps (a "warpgroup") rather than individual warps. WGMMA performs larger matrix multiplications in fewer instructions, reducing instruction overhead and improving tensor core utilization. FA-3 restructures its inner loop to dispatch WGMMA instructions rather than the Ampere-style per-warp MMA instructions used in FA-2.

FA-3 optionally accepts FP8 inputs (E4M3 or E5M2 format), performing the QK^T matmul in FP8 and accumulating into FP32. H100 FP8 tensor cores deliver 2× the throughput of FP16 tensor cores (1979 TFLOPS vs 989 TFLOPS), and FA-3's FP8 path approaches 1300 TFLOPS on some workloads. Precision loss is controlled through per-block scaling factors.

The FP8 TFLOPS figures use H100's FP8 tensor core peak, which is 2× FP16 peak. The effective precision impact (perplexity degradation, task accuracy) depends on calibration quality; FA-3 FP8 is appropriate for inference but requires careful per-block scaling for training.
Using FA-2 and FA-3 in Practice

import torch
from flash_attn import flash_attn_func

# Verify flash-attn version
import flash_attn
print(f"flash-attn version: {flash_attn.__version__}")
# flash-attn >= 2.0 → FA-2 kernel
# flash-attn >= 3.0 (separate package) → FA-3 kernel on H100

# FA-2: standard fp16/bf16 on Ampere+
B, N, H, D = 2, 4096, 16, 64
q = torch.randn(B, N, H, D, dtype=torch.float16, device="cuda")
k = torch.randn(B, N, H, D, dtype=torch.float16, device="cuda")
v = torch.randn(B, N, H, D, dtype=torch.float16, device="cuda")
out = flash_attn_func(q, k, v, causal=True)
# out: (B, N, H, D)

# FA-3 (requires flash-attn-3 package, H100 GPU)
try:
    from flash_attn_interface import flash_attn_func as flash_attn3_func
    out3 = flash_attn3_func(q, k, v, causal=True)
    print("FA-3 kernel active")
except ImportError:
    print("FA-3 not available; using FA-2")

# HuggingFace: FA-2 via attn_implementation
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    "meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B",
    torch_dtype=torch.bfloat16,
    attn_implementation="flash_attention_2",
    device_map="auto",
)
# Note: HuggingFace does not yet expose FA-3 via attn_implementation.
# FA-3 is accessed through the flash_attn_interface package directly.

# PyTorch 2.x SDPA — automatically selects best available kernel
import torch.nn.functional as F
with torch.backends.cuda.sdp_kernel(enable_flash=True, enable_math=False, enable_mem_efficient=False):
    qb = q.transpose(1, 2)  # (B, H, N, D)
    kb = k.transpose(1, 2)
    vb = v.transpose(1, 2)
    out_sdpa = F.scaled_dot_product_attention(qb, kb, vb, is_causal=True)
# PyTorch 2.0+ dispatches to flash kernel if CUDA >= 8.0, fp16/bf16
PyTorch silently falls back to slower kernels when FlashAttention requirements are not met. This code checks which kernel will be dispatched and measures actual throughput:

import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F
import time

def check_sdp_backend(
    B: int = 2, N: int = 2048, H: int = 16, D: int = 64,
    dtype: torch.dtype = torch.float16,
):
    """Report which SDPA backend will be used and its throughput."""
    device = "cuda"
    q = torch.randn(B, H, N, D, device=device, dtype=dtype)
    k = torch.randn(B, H, N, D, device=device, dtype=dtype)
    v = torch.randn(B, H, N, D, device=device, dtype=dtype)

    cap = torch.cuda.get_device_capability()
    cc = cap[0] * 10 + cap[1]
    device_name = torch.cuda.get_device_name()

    print(f"GPU: {device_name}")
    print(f"Compute capability: {cap[0]}.{cap[1]}")
    print(f"dtype: {dtype}")
    print()

    # Check each backend
    backends = {
        "flash": {"enable_flash": True, "enable_math": False, "enable_mem_efficient": False},
        "mem_efficient": {"enable_flash": False, "enable_math": False, "enable_mem_efficient": True},
        "math": {"enable_flash": False, "enable_math": True, "enable_mem_efficient": False},
    }

    for name, kwargs in backends.items():
        try:
            with torch.backends.cuda.sdp_kernel(**kwargs):
                # Warmup
                for _ in range(3):
                    F.scaled_dot_product_attention(q, k, v, is_causal=True)
                torch.cuda.synchronize()
                t0 = time.perf_counter()
                for _ in range(10):
                    F.scaled_dot_product_attention(q, k, v, is_causal=True)
                torch.cuda.synchronize()
                ms = (time.perf_counter() - t0) / 10 * 1000
            print(f"{name:15s}: {ms:.2f} ms/call  {'← available' if ms < 100 else ''}")
        except RuntimeError as e:
            print(f"{name:15s}: not supported ({str(e)[:60]}...)")  

check_sdp_backend()
# Example output on A100 fp16, N=2048:
# flash          :  0.38 ms/call  ← available  (FA-2)
# mem_efficient  :  0.61 ms/call  ← available  (xformers)
# math           :  2.74 ms/call  ← available  (eager)

# Check if FA-3 is accessible
try:
    from flash_attn_interface import flash_attn_func
    print("FA-3 (flash_attn_interface) available")
except ImportError:
    print("FA-3 not installed. Install: pip install flash-attn-3")
FA-3 won't compile on Ampere; FA-2 via flash-attn >= 2.6 or torch.compile
~1.5× over FA-2 at long N; install flash-attn-3 separately
Requires per-block scale calibration; use for inference only
Slower than flash-attn package but built-in; enable with sdp_kernel(enable_flash=True)
FA-2/3 only support causal + unmasked natively

import torch
import time

def bench_flash(fn, q, k, v, n: int = 20) -> float:
    """Return average call time in ms (Ampere+ GPU, fp16)."""
    for _ in range(3):  # warmup
        fn(q, k, v, causal=True)
    torch.cuda.synchronize()
    t0 = time.perf_counter()
    for _ in range(n):
        fn(q, k, v, causal=True)
    torch.cuda.synchronize()
    return (time.perf_counter() - t0) / n * 1000

if torch.cuda.is_available() and torch.cuda.get_device_capability()[0] >= 8:
    from flash_attn import flash_attn_func as fa2

    print(f"{'N':>6}  {'FA-2 (ms)':>10}  {'Notes'}")
    print("-" * 40)
    for N in [1024, 2048, 4096, 8192, 16384]:
        q = torch.randn(2, N, 16, 64, dtype=torch.float16, device="cuda")
        k = torch.randn(2, N, 16, 64, dtype=torch.float16, device="cuda")
        v = torch.randn(2, N, 16, 64, dtype=torch.float16, device="cuda")
        t_fa2 = bench_flash(fa2, q, k, v)
        print(f"{N:>6}  {t_fa2:>10.2f}")

    # If on H100 with FA-3 installed:
    # from flash_attn_interface import flash_attn_func as fa3
    # t_fa3 = bench_flash(fa3, q, k, v)
    # print(f"FA-3 speedup at N={N}: {t_fa2/t_fa3:.2f}x")
else:
    print("Requires Ampere+ GPU")

# FA-2 typical results on A100 (causal, fp16, B=2, H=16, D=64):
# N=1024:  0.19 ms
# N=2048:  0.38 ms
# N=4096:  1.02 ms
# N=8192:  3.87 ms
# N=16384: 15.1 ms
#
# FA-3 on H100 is ~1.5x faster at each N (TMA pipeline hides HBM latency)
FP8 Precision: Scaling and Accuracy Considerations

FA-3 FP8 mode achieves ~1300 TFLOPS by mapping computations to H100’s FP8 tensor cores, but FP8 has a dynamic range of only ~448 and easily overflows. FA-3 addresses this with per-block scaling: before each tile multiply, it scales the Q/K/V blocks so their absolute maximum fits within the FP8 range, accumulates the result in FP32, and descales before writing back. The online softmax statistics m and ℓ are always kept in FP32 regardless of the compute precision.
When calling FA-3 in FP8, pass
) that contain the per-tensor inverse scale factors produced by your quantization routine:

import torch
from flash_attn import flash_attn_func

def attention_fp8(q_fp8, k_fp8, v_fp8, q_scale, k_scale, v_scale, causal=True):
    """
    q_fp8 / k_fp8 / v_fp8 : torch.float8_e4m3fn tensors
    q_scale, k_scale, v_scale: float32 scale factors (scalar or per-batch/head)
    Returns output in float16.
    """
    # FA-3 FP8 path: only on H100 (sm90a)
    out, lse = flash_attn_func(
        q_fp8, k_fp8, v_fp8,
        causal=causal,
        q_descale=q_scale,
        k_descale=k_scale,
        v_descale=v_scale,
        return_attn_probs=False,
    )
    return out  # float16

def quantize_to_fp8_e4m3(tensor: torch.Tensor):
    """Per-tensor dynamic quantization to float8_e4m3fn."""
    fp8_max = torch.finfo(torch.float8_e4m3fn).max  # 448.0
    amax = tensor.abs().amax()
    scale = amax / fp8_max
    q = (tensor / scale).clamp(-fp8_max, fp8_max).to(torch.float8_e4m3fn)
    return q, scale

# Usage
B, N, H, D = 2, 4096, 32, 128
q = torch.randn(B, N, H, D, dtype=torch.float16, device="cuda")
k = torch.randn(B, N, H, D, dtype=torch.float16, device="cuda")
v = torch.randn(B, N, H, D, dtype=torch.float16, device="cuda")

q8, qs = quantize_to_fp8_e4m3(q)
k8, ks = quantize_to_fp8_e4m3(k)
v8, vs = quantize_to_fp8_e4m3(v)

# ~1300 TFLOPS on H100 SXM5
out_fp8 = attention_fp8(q8, k8, v8, qs, ks, vs)

# Sanity check: compare with fp16 baseline
out_fp16 = flash_attn_func(q, k, v, causal=True)
print(f"Max diff FP8 vs FP16: {(out_fp8.float() - out_fp16.float()).abs().max():.4f}")
FP8 output typically differs from FP16 by < 0.01 absolute error for standard transformer heads with D ≤ 256, but models with extreme activation distributions (e.g., without
) may see larger deviations. Always validate with your model’s eval benchmarks before deploying FP8.
Each FA version has strict environment requirements. Mismatches are the most common source of silent fallback to slow PyTorch attention:
must be compiled against the exact CUDA version that PyTorch was compiled with. Mixing CUDA 11.8 PyTorch with a CUDA 12.x-compiled
wheel causes a silent import error where
succeeds but kernels crash at runtime. Always verify with:

import torch, flash_attn

print(f"PyTorch CUDA: {torch.version.cuda}")
print(f"flash_attn: {flash_attn.__version__}")

# Check that the custom CUDA extension actually loaded
try:
    import flash_attn_2_cuda        # FA-2
    print("FA-2 CUDA extension: OK")
except ImportError:
    print("FA-2 CUDA extension: MISSING — will fall back to PyTorch")

try:
    import flash_attn_3_cuda        # FA-3
    print("FA-3 CUDA extension: OK")
except ImportError:
    print("FA-3 CUDA extension: MISSING — H100 FP8 path unavailable")

# Confirm SM version
if torch.cuda.is_available():
    props = torch.cuda.get_device_properties(0)
    sm = props.major * 10 + props.minor
    print(f"GPU SM: {sm}  ({props.name})")
    print(f"FA-3 eligible: {sm >= 90}")
Head dimension constraints: FA-2 supports D ∈ {16, 32, 64, 96, 128, 160, 192, 224, 256}. FA-3 extends this to D ≤ 512. GQA (grouped-query attention) is supported in both: pass
; the API repeats KV heads internally via
-free tiling. Sliding-window attention and ALiBi positional biases are also first-class in FA-2 (pass

The FlashAttention lineage — FA-1 in 2022, FA-2 in 2023, FA-3 in 2024 — illustrates how algorithmic optimizations must evolve alongside hardware. FA-1 proved that IO-awareness matters more than raw arithmetic reduction. FA-2 showed that even a correct algorithm leaves performance on the table when parallelism and instruction mix don't match the GPU's execution model. FA-3 demonstrates that new hardware features (TMA, WGMMA) require not just using new APIs but redesigning the kernel's pipeline structure from scratch. For practical purposes, use FA-2 on Ampere GPUs via the
in HuggingFace; use FA-3 on H100s if latency is critical and you can tolerate the separate installation.
